stem: The ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) (integers modulo \( [[n]] \)) is a field exactly when \( [[n]] \) is prime (equivalently, exactly when every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse). Is \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) a field? Enter \( 1 \) for yes, \( 0 \) for no.
solution: \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) is a field if and only if \( n \) is prime (for composite \( n=ab \) with \( 1<a,b<n \), \( a \) and \( b \) are nonzero zero divisors, so no field). Checking \( [[n]] \) for primality directly gives the verdict \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the flipped verdict, swapping field and non-field.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.

stem: \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is a field exactly when EVERY nonzero element is a unit, i.e. exactly when the number of units equals \( [[n]]-1 \). How many units (invertible elements) does \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) actually have?
solution: The units of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) are the residues coprime to \( n \); there are \( \varphi(n) \) of them. Here \( \varphi([[n]]) = [[answer]] \). (This equals \( [[n]]-1 \) exactly when \( [[n]] \) is prime, i.e. exactly when \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is a field.)
distractor_rationales: This assumes every nonzero residue is automatically a unit, i.e. assumes \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is always a field regardless of whether \( [[n]] \) is actually prime.
This counts every residue including \( 0 \), which is never a unit.
This guesses that exactly half the residues are units, which is only a coincidence for some moduli.
This subtracts two instead of computing the actual totient via the prime factorization of \( [[n]] \).
This is one more than the correct unit count.
This is one fewer than the correct unit count.
This is two more than the correct unit count.

stem: \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) fails to be a field/integral domain exactly when it has a nonzero element with no multiplicative inverse (a zero divisor). How many of the \( [[n]]-1 \) nonzero elements of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) are NOT units? (A value of \( 0 \) here means \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) IS a field.)
solution: Of the \( [[n]] \) residues mod \( [[n]] \), exactly \( \varphi([[n]]) \) are units and one is \( 0 \); every other nonzero residue is a non-unit (zero divisor). So the non-unit count is \( ([[n]]-1) - \varphi([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of UNITS \( \varphi([[n]]) \), the complement of what was asked.
This still includes \( 0 \) in the non-unit count; the question asks only about the \( n-1 \) NONZERO elements.
This claims every nonzero element fails to be a unit, as if \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) had no units at all.
This is one less than the unit count \( \varphi([[n]]) \), not the non-unit count.
This is one more than the correct non-unit count.
This is one fewer than the correct non-unit count.
This is two more than the correct non-unit count.

stem: Of the two moduli \( n = [[n1]] \) and \( n = [[n2]] \), for how many of them is \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) a field (equivalently, an integral domain, since \( \mathbb{Z}_n \) is finite)?

answer_spec.expr: Piecewise((1, isprime([[n1]])), (0, True)) + Piecewise((1, isprime([[n2]])), (0, True))

solution: \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) is a field exactly when \( n \) is prime. Testing \( [[n1]] \) and \( [[n2]] \) for primality individually and adding the results gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes both moduli give a field without testing either for primality.
This is not a valid count out of two candidates — the answer must be 0, 1, or 2.
This is not a valid count at all — the answer must be 0, 1, or 2.
This assumes neither modulus gives a field without testing either for primality.
This assumes exactly one modulus gives a field without testing either for primality.
